The astral body is a bit like a little sun, a fire that breaks everything down (thus astral —for it corresponds to the stars and the cosmos). Finally, we have an I or ego body , which allows us to consider all these processes in thinking. The ego body is the principle in us that allows us to think about thinking, feeling, and doing. Of course, the object-body isn’t ever actually isolated from the other bodies. Even when we sleep—the time the object-body interacts with the etheric body and our cells repair skin and hair—the principles of the other bodies are still in action. It’s a bit like water, which is never truly just H 2 O, but always mixed with other elements in gases and sediment. In trying to conceptualize the object-body separately and in its relation with the other principles at work, blending both the scientific and experiential perspective, we can begin to build a phenomenology of it. To get a full picture of the object-body, we must first consider the scientific viewpoint: the object-body doesn’t act solely though our conscious will. The heart beats, the lungs contract and expand, and our blood moves through us. The body, it is often said, has a mind of its own, and its actions intersect with experience. Anyone who has ever had an erection in public will know immediately what I’m talking about. When it happens, the independent will of the body is glaringly obvious. Then again, it’s not only the penis that reacts to sexual stimulation. We also sweat, out hearts race, we may get a little jump in our stomachs. In fact, the body’s sexual response is often how we know we’re attracted to someone. We may be surprised to find ourselves aroused, but there it is: a draw toward another. This draw can be sustained, and often is. When we see someone we’re attracted to for a second or third time, when we first start dating or after we have sex, the draw stays there. Scientists have widely agreed that there is a combination of factors—including hormones, dopamine, and adrenaline—that work in conjunction with this draw. The attraction becomes very powerful, allowing us to forgive faults we might not normally. Anything that normally annoys us becomes endearing while this draw is sustained. The body’s will makes us extremely kind. But our attitude to this kindness is often flippant. Cognitive scientists and neuroscientists may refer to the above chemical changes as the cause of it all; there’s nothing special about that love stuff, really, just chemicals. Evolutionary psychologists might refer it back to advantageous mating behaviors, leaving out present-day context. In popular culture, we might say, “that’s just infatuation.” We might say that being attracted to someone because of his or her appearance is “shallow.” If someone acts on this initial attraction, we might refer to her or him as a “slut.” A contradiction, then: we love the feeling that the will of the object-body brings, but we don’t hold it in high regard. We think of it as somehow fake. What if we took it seriously? What if, instead of measuring it up to other experiences, we reversed our ethic and held this infatuation stage up as the standard? We would see then that it’s not that these initial feelings are false or fake, it’s that we don’t feel them enough . In other words, we aren’t normally as forgiving and adoring of other people as we are in the initial stages of attraction. What if we were? What if we were as loving and forgiving in our lives as we were while we were sexually aroused? 5 Of course, this isn’t totally possible, because thinking and feeling and growth all interact with the will of the body, and a much more complicated picture of other people eventually emerges. We might stop being sexually aroused by people after a time. Where we once found their accents or the way they chew their food charming, we now find them annoying. This complex picture is also good, because it presents us with an opportunity for intention. What we once did automatically, we have to learn to do consciously. Where once we allowed faults, we must learn to forgive. Where once we couldn’t stop having sex, we have to get ourselves in the mood. We must act with intention. 6 But where did we learn what it means to be at peace with flaws and to love unreservedly? Our first lived-out lesson in that—in loving unconditionally, in excitement in the other person, in forgiveness—comes from the object-body. II The first time I masturbated thinking of a man, I was barely a teenager. I’d masturbated before, but I never really understood why—it was just a feeling contained in myself. I’d push myself into my mattress and consider the strange, warm feeling. Waves up my chest and in my spine, a peaceful feeling afterward. It was unrelated to anything but me. But then my body began to teach me something. I went to the beach with my family and saw my older stepbrother’s friend in the shower. Through the clouded glass of the shower door, I saw his form, the color of his skin, his legs, what must have been his arms, his ass. There were no clear lines, there were shapes and color. I looked at him and saw what was there. I felt inside of me something entirely new, the coalition of light and sound and this … feeling. My body was going crazy, and I had no idea why. My object-body was wiser than the rest of me, it knew things I didn’t, and it was responding to someone else’s body. For gay men, lesbians, and bisexuals (the GLB community), the object-body is a constant teacher of integrity. 7 For many in the GLB community, internal struggle shows up early in life.
